name: Brent Crude
summary: WHIPLASH DAY: Morning pullback to $107.71 on Trump 'agreed to most' was FALSE DAWN. Brent reversed violently to $116 intraday, settling ~$115.27 after Trump told FT 'my favourite thing is to take the oil in Iran.' A $12/bbl swing in 18 hours from one person's contradictory statements. Day 23+ above $100. Brent +51% in March — largest monthly surge on record (IEA). IEA: 'biggest oil shock in history.'

price_snapshots.delta_context: Last price before US-Israel strikes on Iran
Surged ~15% on first trading day after attacks
Intraday peak on energy infrastructure escalation
Iraq cut production from 3.3M to 900K bpd

price_snapshots.trigger: Trump announced 5-day strike pause, claimed Iran talks
price_snapshots.delta_context: Fell from $112 to $103 in single session. Biggest single-day drop since war began.

price_snapshots.trigger: Rebound as Iran denies talks, strikes continue, Houthi escalation signal
price_snapshots.delta_context: Partial reversal of yesterday's crash. Market realizing the 'talks' may be fiction. Chevron CEO says prices 'not fully priced in.'

price_snapshots.trigger: Houthi war entry + Tangsiri assassination + toll booth + ceasefire rejection
price_snapshots.delta_context: Surged from $103.56 (Mar 24) to $112.57. Day 21+ above $100. Goldman estimates $14-18/bbl risk premium.

price_snapshots.trigger: First major pullback — Trump diplomacy claims + profit-taking
price_snapshots.delta_context: Dropped 4.3% from $112.57. First significant decline since war began. Built on claims not confirmed de-escalation. Pentagon preparing ground ops simultaneously.
Morning pullback on 'agreed to most' diplomacy claim reversed within hours by 'take the oil' FT interview. $12/bbl swing in 18 hours.

signals.content: Oil surged faster than during any other conflict in recent history following US-Israel strikes on Iran and Iranian retaliation across Gulf states.
Brent crossed $100/bbl for first time in four years as Hormuz closure entered second week with no resolution in sight.
Crude topped $112 after Iraq declared force majeure on all foreign-operated oilfields and drones struck two Kuwaiti refineries. Fifth straight weekly gain.
US temporarily lifted some sanctions on Iranian oil — Treasury Sec Bessent said expected to add ~140M barrels to global market. Major policy reversal suggesting price sensitivity.

Brent crashed 8.4% to $102.73/bbl after Trump announced 5-day postponement of strikes on Iranian energy infrastructure, citing 'productive conversations' with Iran. Biggest single-day drop since war began. Iran denied any talks.

Brent rebounded 3.6% to $103.56 as market digested Iran's categorical denial of any talks, continued missile strikes on Israel, and Houthi announcement of entering the war threatening Red Sea closure. Chevron CEO Mike Wirth said effects are 'not fully priced in.' VIX rose 4.9% to 27.43, signaling returning fear. The rebound suggests yesterday's de-escalation optimism was premature.

Brent surged to $112.57 (+4.2% on day, +59.1% monthly). Multiple drivers: Iran ceasefire rejection (Mar 25), Tangsiri assassination (Mar 26), IRGC toll booth formalization (Mar 27), Houthi war entry (Mar 28). Goldman estimates $14-18/bbl geopolitical risk premium. Oil in steep backwardation. Day 21+ above $100.

Brent pulled back 4.3% to $107.71 — first major decline since war began. Driven by: (1) Trump claim Iran 'agreed to most of' 15-point demands, (2) Islamabad quad with Pakistan offering to host US-Iran talks, (3) profit-taking near $119 peak. Pullback is built on CLAIMS, not confirmed de-escalation. Pentagon simultaneously preparing Kharg Island ground ops. If Iran denies or ground ops proceed, pullback reverses. (Sources: Investing.com, CNN, Bloomberg, NPR)

EVENING UPDATE: Morning pullback to $107.71 was completely reversed. Trump's FT interview — 'my favourite thing is to take the oil in Iran' and Kharg Island seizure talk — drove Brent from $104 to $116 intraday, settling ~$115.27 (+2.4% from Friday $112.57). CNN headlined 'Oil prices top $116.' Additionally, Iran struck Kuwait desalination plant and Saudi intercepted 5 missiles — compounding supply fear. Brent +51% in March — on track for largest monthly surge on record. IEA: 'biggest oil shock in history.' The 18-hour arc (diplomacy claim → oil seizure rhetoric) encapsulates why claims-based oil moves are inherently fragile. (Sources: CNN Business, CNBC, Bloomberg, FT, Al Jazeera)

edges.context: ~20% of global oil transits Hormuz. Closure is the primary driver of the price spike from $70 to $119.
India imports 85-90% of crude. At $103/bbl, massive additional import cost. Direct pressure on CAD, rupee, inflation.
Higher oil = more dollar demand for imports = rupee depreciation. INR at 93.80/USD.
Oil spike transmits war to monetary policy. Oil above $100 = inflation stays elevated = Fed can't cut. All three major central banks (Fed, ECB, RBI) now on hold in stagflation trap.
Goldman: 20% Brent rise cuts Asian earnings by 2%. Higher oil = margin compression for most companies.
OPEC+ agreed modest 206K bpd increase but most barrels stranded if Hormuz stays shut.
Oil and gas prices linked. LNG contracts often indexed to oil. Dual pressure on energy importers.
Oil above $100 = India inflation = RBI can't cut rates to support growth. Stagflation trap.

trigger_points.mechanism: India's import bill rises ~$25B annually per $10 oil increase. Sustained $100+ oil widens CAD to ~3.5% GDP.
Would signal supply normalization. India relief: lower import bill, rupee stabilization, room for RBI rate cuts.

trigger_points.condition: Brent spikes above $130 on Houthi Red Sea entry
trigger_points.mechanism: Double chokepoint (Hormuz + Red Sea) would remove majority of Gulf export capacity. Would trigger emergency SPR releases, possible recession.
